metadata.split_role: Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, and oddball bolete records.
metadata.notes: Physical data split only. Species record bodies were preserved; each canonical slug appears in exactly one base mushroom file.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Admirable Bolete. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from July through September in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.

records.culinary_uses: Reported edible when young and in good condition, but treat as a confident-ID bolete rather than a beginner mushroom.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Admirable Bolete. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Usually not staining; occasionally weak blue bruising on pores
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; often July through October in the Great Lakes window.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.edibility_detail: Best approached as an edible-with-caution species: confirm pine association, very long ridged stem, yellow pores, and lack of dangerous red-pored traits.
records.field_identification: Mycorrhizal with pines. Look for a reddish-brown to brown cap, yellow pore surface, very long ridged to nearly reticulate stem, prominent white basal mycelium, and a cap margin that can project beyond the pore surface.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Aureoboletus projectellus
use_links.notes: Pine association, field marks, season, and staining notes.

records.edibility_notes: Reported edible when young and in good condition, but treat as a confident-ID bolete rather than a beginner mushroom. Best approached as an edible-with-caution species: confirm pine association, very long ridged stem, yellow pores, and lack of dangerous red-pored traits.
curation_notes: Minimal species seed. Added from the Michigan bolete audit for the clean app so the bolete section is more complete; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review. Baseline audit pass added lane, seasonality, commonness, food quality, host filtering, and comparison notes.
This record still needs species-level images and source links, but the core field-guide traits are now scaffolded for sorting and filtering.

edible_use.notes: Reported edible when young and in good condition, but treat as a confident-ID bolete rather than a beginner mushroom. Best approached as an edible-with-caution species: confirm pine association, very long ridged stem, yellow pores, and lack of dangerous red-pored traits.

records.culinary_uses: Not collected for meals in this guide. Keep as an identification record until local use and species limits are checked.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Aureoboletus Roxanae. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Pores usually not bruising; flesh may stain bluish
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; species-level local timing needs more checking.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Dry, smooth to slightly wrinkled cap; yellow to greenish-yellow pore surface that usually does not bruise; yellowish stem with reddish colorations and tiny dots; flesh may stain bluish in some collections. Reported under hardwoods in eastern North America.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert Boletus key: Aureoboletus roxanae
use_links.notes: Hardwood association, yellow/greenish pores, dotted stem, and non-bruising pore surface.

records.edibility_notes: Yellow-pored, dotted-stem Aureoboletus/Xerocomus-type bolete. Do not infer kitchen use from nearby bolete relatives.
curation_notes: Minimal species seed. Added from the Michigan bolete audit for the clean app so the bolete section is more complete; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review. Baseline audit pass added lane, seasonality, commonness, food quality, host filtering, and comparison notes.
This record still needs species-level images and source links, but the core field-guide traits are now scaffolded for sorting and filtering.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Old Man of the Woods. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; often July through October.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
pore_color: Whitish to gray pores becoming blackish
cap_surface: Woolly black scales over gray-white base

records.edibility_detail: Not a prime edible. Use only young, firm specimens if at all; older specimens are poor-textured and unappealing.
records.field_identification: Distinctive gray-black woolly/scaly bolete with a shaggy cap and stem. Pores start whitish-gray and darken; cut flesh turns pinkish or red, then blackens over time.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Old Man of the Woods
use_links.notes: Scaly cap, pore color, hardwood association, and staining progression.

records.edibility_notes: Edible by some accounts when young, but usually poor quality; included mainly because it is distinctive and useful for ID practice. Not a prime edible.
curation_notes: Minimal species seed. Added from the Michigan bolete audit for the clean app so the bolete section is more complete; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review.

edible_use.notes: Edible by some accounts when young, but usually poor quality; included mainly because it is distinctive and useful for ID practice. Not a prime edible.

records.duplicate_note: Duplicate Old Man of the Woods record kept only as a legacy slug alias.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as food because it grows directly on Scleroderma earthballs, which are poisonous.
records.notes: Grows directly on earthballs rather than from plain soil or wood. Look for small yellow-brown pored caps attached to the surface of an earthball; that parasitic habit is the main field clue.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Parasitic Bolete. Added in the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Little to no staining; may stain dirty brown slowly
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, when earthballs are present.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.general_notes: Useful as a caution/curiosity species because the growth habit is distinctive: a bolete growing on another fungus.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Small yellow-brown bolete that grows directly from common earthballs (Scleroderma), usually the most important ID feature. Pores are yellow to olive and the stem is small and often curved.
records.edibility_notes: The mushroom itself is sometimes described as edible, but this guide treats it as avoid/not recommended because of its poisonous earthball host.
curation_notes: Minimal species seed. Added during the bolete expansion pass for the clean app; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review.
